real_world_layer: Cusumano/Gawer/Yoffie, The Business of Platforms; Parker et al.
title: Platform Economics: Network Effects & Winner-Take-Most
quick_definition: The sector's biggest businesses aren't just products — they're multi-sided platforms (★G297): businesses whose real product is the connection between groups (developers ↔ users, merchants ↔ shoppers, drivers ↔ riders). Platforms grow on network effects (★G298) — each new participant makes the thing more valuable for everyone else — and keep what they win through switching costs (★G299). Together, these are why tech markets so often tip to one or two winners.
explainer_covers: The three-"platforms" callout, in-node: HF's ★G132 pod platform and VC's ★G216 platform team are organizational uses of the word; this is the economic one — Cusumano's subject. A reader arriving from either module should re-set the term before continuing.

★G297, assembled (Cusumano Ch. 1/3): the pipeline firm makes a thing and sells it downstream; the platform stands between groups and gets paid for the match. The two clean types — transaction platforms (marketplaces: app stores, ride-hailing, payments) and innovation platforms (foundations others build on: operating systems, cloud platforms, the "Falcon platform" sense in the dossier's 10-Ks) — plus the hybrids that run both. The launch problem every platform faces: chicken-and-egg (no users without developers, no developers without users).

★G298, built up gently: a phone is useless alone and priceless when everyone has one — value that comes from other users, not the product. Direct (same-side) vs. indirect (cross-side: more merchants → more shoppers → more merchants) effects; and the honest caveat that they run in reverse too (emptying networks collapse). This is the microeconomic engine behind VC's power law (★G201 — VC z1.2 names the mechanism; this is where it's actually taught) and behind the exchange moats sector-financials Z2.3 gestures at.

★G299, the keeper: what it would cost a customer — money, time, risk, retraining, data migration — to leave. Enterprise software's quiet superpower (ripping out a deployed system is a career risk; hence NRR > 100%, Z3.2), device ecosystems' visible one (the Apple model — App Store, OS lock-in, services attach), and the platform's compounding one (your data, your integrations, your habits). Lock-in is the mechanism that converts today's win into tomorrow's pricing power — the sector's native moat material, distinct from network effects and often mistaken for them.

Winner-take-most, with conditions (Cusumano Ch. 2 — the discipline): markets tip when network effects are strong, multi-homing is hard (you can't cheaply use two), and differentiation/niches are scarce. When multi-homing is easy (food delivery on two apps), platforms compete forever at thin margins — "platform" is not a magic word, and most platform launches fail on pricing, trust, or timing. The tipped-market endgame — dominance, complements squeezed, regulators arriving — is Z5.1's story.

connects_to.note: the power law — this node supplies its engine
the dollar as a network-effect asset

connects_to_raw: ★G132/★G216 (disambiguate — the three platforms), ★G201, Z2.1, Z1.2, Z5.1, `sector-financials.z2.3`, `macro-economics.z5.5`.

title: Keeping Score in Subscription Land: ARR, NRR & the Compounding Base
quick_definition: A subscription business doesn't think in "sales" — it thinks in a base of recurring revenue it owns and grows. ARR (★G300) is the size of that base: subscription revenue that recurs each year unless something changes. NRR (★G301) is what the base does on its own: what last year's customers spend this year, expansions netted against cancellations — the single number that says whether the machine compounds without any new customers at all.
explainer_covers: Why the scoreboard changed (Tzuo — the operator's view, not the analyst's): when revenue recurs, the question stops being "what did we sell this quarter?" and becomes "how big is the base, and is it growing or leaking?" Subscription operators literally run the company on this dashboard — the analyst use comes later (Z4).

★G300 ARR, plainly: the annualized value of active subscriptions — a run-rate, not an accounting number. It deliberately differs from GAAP revenue, which recognizes subscriptions ratably over time (the deferred-revenue timing lag Z4.2 reads); ARR answers "how big is the machine now." Live exhibits from the dossier's clean filings: Palo Alto and CrowdStrike both lead their 10-Ks with ARR.

★G301 NRR, built up in layers: start with last year's customer cohort's revenue = 100. Some cancel or shrink — churn — leaving gross retention (CrowdStrike discloses this too, "dollar-based gross retention"). Some buy more seats and products — expansion. Net the two: NRR. Above 100% means the installed base grows by itself — the business compounds before the first new logo — which is the property that separates great software businesses from good ones, and it's earned by lock-in plus land-and-expand (★G299, Z3.1). Elite enterprise software prints 110–130%.

The seam, stated in-node: VC z3.4 uses this same vocabulary as traction evidence for pre-scale startups (retention curves flattening = product-market fit; CAC/LTV as the unit-economics test — taught there, back-linked here). This node is the other end of the telescope: the same metrics as the reported operating model of public software companies. ★G215 is the bridge.

The rest of the dashboard, in one honest pass: bookings (deals signed), billings (invoiced), RPO (contracted-but-not-yet-recognized — the filings' forward-revenue line, read in Z4.2), gross margin on the subscription line (★G293's fingerprint), and the growth-vs-profitability tension the whole dashboard feeds — which the analyst compresses into one number in Z4.1 (★G302). None of these mint globals; they live in ARR/NRR's orbit.

connects_to.note: the startup lens — the seam's far side
the model that created the metrics
what the analyst does with these

connects_to_raw: `venture-capital.z3.4` (the startup lens), ★G215, Z2.1 (★G294), Z3.1 (★G299), Z4.1, Z4.2, ★G183.
